explicit_text: My name is Paul Mitsuo Kurose, born on September 26, 1956, in Seattle, Washington. I identify as Japanese American and have always been very aware of my racial identity, shaped by my family history and experiences growing up. I am deeply connected to the Japanese American community and the Central District of Seattle where I was raised. Professionally, I work at North Seattle College and am involved in various community and union groups, including the American Federation of Teachers.
implicit_text: My name is Paul Mitsuo Kurose, born on September 26, 1956, in Seattle, Washington. I identify as Japanese American and have always been very aware of my racial identity, shaped by my family history and experiences growing up. I am deeply connected to the Japanese American community and the Central District of Seattle where I was raised. Professionally, I work at a community college in North Seattle and am involved in various community and union groups, including a local teachers' union.

explicit_text: Seattle is where I was born and raised, specifically in the Central District, which was a diverse and predominantly people of color community. Growing up there in the 1960s, we faced racial restrictions, like the 34th Avenue red line that affected housing. The city has been central to my life and activism, from my family's struggles with housing discrimination to my mother’s work in education and social justice. Seattle remains the place where I am deeply connected to community and cultural identity.
implicit_text: This city is where I was born and raised, specifically in the Central District, which was a diverse and predominantly people of color community. Growing up there in the 1960s, we faced racial restrictions, like the 34th Avenue red line that affected housing. The city has been central to my life and activism, from my family's struggles with housing discrimination to my mother’s work in education and social justice. It remains the place where I am deeply connected to community and cultural identity.

explicit_text: The Japanese American Citizens League has been an important organization in my family's life, especially for my mother, Aki Kurose, and my older sister Ruth Ann. They were involved in the JACL's activism, particularly around the redress movement for World War II incarceration. Today, I participate in the JACL scholarship committee that awards a scholarship in my mom’s name and engage in activities at a middle school named after her. The JACL's evolution into a progressive force for justice has been meaningful to me and my family.
implicit_text: An important organization in my family's life, especially for my mother, Aki, and my older sister Ruth Ann, was a longstanding Japanese American civil rights group. They were involved in its activism, particularly around the redress movement for World War II incarceration. Today, I participate in the scholarship committee of this group that awards a scholarship in my mom’s name and engage in activities at a middle school named after her. The organization's evolution into a progressive force for justice has been meaningful to me and my family.

explicit_text: I work at North Seattle College, part of the Seattle College District, where I am involved in several affinity groups including the Asian Pacific Islander affinity group and the BIPOC caucus within the American Federation of Teachers local 1789. This workplace community has been a place where I continue my commitment to racial and social justice. My experience as a teacher and union member here connects me to broader struggles for equity in education.

explicit_text: I am an active member of the American Federation of Teachers, local 1789, within the Seattle College District. Through this union, I participate in the BIPOC caucus which focuses on issues affecting people of color in education. This involvement allows me to advocate for equity and support colleagues in the community college system. It’s an important part of my professional and social engagement.

explicit_text: My mother, Aki Kurose, was a remarkable activist and educator deeply involved in social justice causes in Seattle. She was a Quaker and peace activist who also worked on fair housing protests in the 1960s and helped launch Project Head Start in Seattle. She later became a teacher at Martin Luther King Elementary School and was recognized with a Presidential Award for her work. After her passing, a middle school formerly named Sharpless was renamed in her honor, and the Japanese American Citizens League awards a scholarship in her name.
implicit_text: My mother was a remarkable activist and educator deeply involved in social justice causes in Seattle. She was a Quaker and peace activist who also worked on fair housing protests in the 1960s and helped launch an early childhood education program in Seattle. She later became a teacher at an elementary school named after Martin Luther King and was recognized with a Presidential Award for her work. After her passing, a middle school formerly known by another name was renamed in her honor, and a Japanese American civil rights group awards a scholarship in her name.

explicit_text: World War II had a profound impact on my family and community, especially through the Japanese American incarceration. My mother and family were involved in activism to gain formal acknowledgment and redress for the injustices committed during that time. The Japanese American Citizens League, which my family supported, evolved to become a key player in the redress movement. This history is a significant part of my racial identity and community consciousness.
implicit_text: The global conflict of the early 1940s had a profound impact on my family and community, especially through the incarceration of Japanese Americans. My mother and family were involved in activism to gain formal acknowledgment and redress for the injustices committed during that time. The Japanese American civil rights group, which my family supported, evolved to become a key player in the redress movement. This history is a significant part of my racial identity and community consciousness.

explicit_text: The redress movement was a critical cause for my family, especially my mother and sister, who worked through the Japanese American Citizens League to seek formal government acknowledgment for the wrongs done during World War II incarceration. This movement marked a turning point in Japanese American activism and helped bring justice and recognition to our community. My involvement today continues to honor that legacy through scholarship committees and community education.

My involvement today continues to honor that legacy through scholarship committees and community education.

explicit_text: My mother, Aki Kurose, was a Quaker, and her faith deeply influenced her peace activism and social justice work. Being a Quaker shaped her commitment to nonviolence and community service, which was evident in her involvement with fair housing protests and education initiatives in Seattle. This religious identity was a core part of her life and activism, impacting our family and community.

explicit_text: 34th Avenue in Seattle was a significant boundary in my neighborhood, known as a red line that racially restricted where my parents could buy a home. In 1961, my parents faced discrimination when realtors from Lake Washington Realty refused to sell them a house on the other side of 34th Avenue. This experience was a catalyst for my mother’s involvement in fair housing activism during the 1960s. The 34th Avenue red line symbolizes the racial barriers our family and community confronted.
implicit_text: A particular avenue in Seattle was a significant boundary in my neighborhood, known as a red line that racially restricted where my parents could buy a home. In 1961, my parents faced discrimination when realtors from a local company refused to sell them a house on the other side of this avenue. This experience was a catalyst for my mother’s involvement in fair housing activism during the 1960s. The red line on this avenue symbolizes the racial barriers our family and community confronted.

explicit_text: Lake Washington Realty was the real estate company that refused to sell a house to my parents in 1961 because it was on the other side of the 34th Avenue red line, a racially restrictive boundary in Seattle. This discriminatory act was part of the systemic racism my family faced growing up. It motivated my mother to become active in fair housing protests and social justice efforts in the city. This experience is a stark reminder of the barriers faced by Japanese Americans and other people of color.

This experience is a stark reminder of the barriers faced by Japanese Americans and other people of color.

explicit_text: My mother worked with Project Head Start when it first began in Seattle, collaborating with Dorothy Hollingsworth and Daisy Dawson to get the program off the ground. This early childhood education initiative was important in providing opportunities for children in the community. Her involvement demonstrated her commitment to education and social justice, which influenced my own path as an educator.

explicit_text: Dorothy Hollingsworth was a key leader in launching Project Head Start in Seattle, working closely with my mother, Aki Kurose, and Daisy Dawson. She played a major role in establishing this important early childhood education program. My mother’s collaboration with Dorothy was part of her broader activism and dedication to improving educational opportunities for children in our community.
implicit_text: One key leader in launching the early childhood education program in Seattle worked closely with my mother and another community leader. She played a major role in establishing this important program. My mother’s collaboration with her was part of her broader activism and dedication to improving educational opportunities for children in our community.

explicit_text: Daisy Dawson, also known as Daisy Tibbs Dawson, worked alongside my mother and Dorothy Hollingsworth to help start Project Head Start in Seattle. She was instrumental in the early development of this program aimed at supporting young children’s education. My mother’s partnership with Daisy reflects the collaborative efforts of community leaders to advance social justice through education.
implicit_text: Another community leader worked alongside my mother and a key figure to help start the early childhood education program in Seattle. explicit_text: My mother taught kindergarten and first grade at Martin Luther King Elementary School in Seattle, where Louise McKinney was the principal. This school was part of her career as an educator and her commitment to serving the community. The school was located in the Central District, the neighborhood where we grew up, and was connected to other community leaders like Reverend Samuel B. McKinney, Louise’s husband.

Her leadership helped shape the environment where my mother contributed as a teacher in Seattle’s Central District.

His role added to the rich tapestry of leadership in the neighborhood where we lived and worked.

explicit_text: Sharpless Middle School in Seattle was renamed in honor of my mother, Aki Kurose, after her passing, recognizing her lifelong dedication to education and social justice. The school was formerly known as Sharpless Junior High and the renaming was supported by many community members from diverse backgrounds. This honor reflects the impact my mother had on the Seattle community and her legacy as an educator and activist.
